coredata.dc:title: Stimuli-responsive pressure–strain sensor-based conductive hydrogel for alleviated non-alcoholic fatty liver disease by scavenging reactive oxygen species in adipose tissue
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shit, Arnab
Park, Sujeong
Lee, Yunki
Ryplida, Benny
Morgan, Nyssa
Jang, Young C.
Jin, Eun-Jung
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description:
A visible light- and reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive pressure/strain sensor based on carbon dot (CD)-loaded conductive hydrogel was developed for detecting high-fat diet (HFD) and preventing the risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. The designed nanoparticle consisted of a diselenide polymer dot (dsPD) loaded with a visible light-responsive CD to form dsPD@CD (DSCD). The influence of visible light irradiation and ROS on DSCD facilitated the electron transport, enhancing the conductivity of DSCD-embedded hydrogel (DSCD hydrogel) from 1.3 to 35.9 mS/m. Alternatively, the tensile modulus of the DSCD hydrogel enhanced to 223 % after light-induced ROS treatment, which simultaneously impacted the capacitive response (120 %). The hydrogel implantation into inguinal white adipose tissue of HFD mice showed 82 % higher conductivity and 83 % enhanced pressure sensing response to HFD-generated high ROS levels compared with the normal diet-fed mice. Additionally, the ROS scavenging activity of DSCD hydrogel was confirmed by the downregulation of ROS-responsive genes, such as Sod2, Nrf2, and catalase (Cat) in murine primary hepatocytes isolated from fatty liver-induced mice. In addition, in vivo animal studies also confirmed the suppression of hepatic lipogenesis, as shown by decreased Pparγ and Fasn expression and hypertrophy of adipocytes in HFD mice. The distinguishable real-time wireless resistance response observed with pressure sensing indicates the potential application of the device for monitoring the risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.
Statement of Significance A visible-light-induced ROS-responsive carbon dot-loaded conductive hydrogel was developed for the detection of HFD-induced alterations in ROS levels by evaluating the conductivity and electrochemical responses with applied pressure/strain. The implanted hydrogel facilitates the recovery of the inflated adipocytes induced by NAFLD, which reduces fat accumulation in the liver, preventing the risk of NAFLD. Real-time detection based on the resistance response during local compression of the hydrogel is possibly performed utilizing a wireless sensing device, demonstrating the ease of NAFLD monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diselenide
ROS scavenger
Carbon dot
Stimuli-responsive hydrogel
Pressure sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of DSCD-loaded PVA-HPC hydrogels (DSCD hydrogel)
Monitoring pressure and strain response using a wireless sensing system
In vivo electrochemical and pressure sensing performance of DSCD hydrogel
Fabrication of visible light- and ROS-responsive DSCD hydrogel
Surface morphology, optical, electrical, and mechanical properties of DSCD hydrogel
Electrical resistance changes in response to mechanical deformation (tensile and compression)
In vitro study of DSCD hydrogel using NCD and HFD hepatocytes
In vivo study of ROS scavenging behavior and free fatty acid reduction performance of DSCD hydrogel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) , funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. ( RS-2023-00207925 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 to S.Y Park and 2021R1I1A3041149 to E-J Jin).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of visible light- and ROS-responsive DSCD hydrogel, and the effect during implantation onto iWAT in response to the HFD-generated ROS condition. (b) DLS and (c) PL spectra of the DSCD depend on 9 h of visible light, H2O2, and visible light and H2O2 simultaneous treatment. (d) XPS narrow spectra of the Se 3d after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. TEM image of the DSCD (e) before and (f) after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. AFM image of the DSCD hydrogel-coated Si-wafer surface (g) before and (h) after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. The control experiment was performed without H2O2 and visible irradiation.

(a) EIS spectra, (b) tensile property, (c) visual stretchability, (d) strain sweep measurements at a constant frequency of 1 Hz, and (e) dynamic rheological test data was obtained by cyclically varying the strain] of the DSCD hydrogel after 9 h of treatment individually with visible light, H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. (f) The digital image of the self-healing of the DSCD hydrogel. (g) Tensile property of the DSCD hydrogel before and after healing. The control experiment was performed without the H2O2 and visible irradiation.

Performances of pressure-strain sensors assembled using DSCD hydrogels after 9 h of individual treatment with visible light and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. (a) Changes in capacitance as a function of strain and linear fitting curve. (b) Cyclic response of the strain with different loadings. (c) Cyclic stability of finger bending motion before and after visible light treatment. Capacitance response of (d) finger bending and (e) finger pressing motions. (f) Cyclic stability of the finger bending motion. (g) Wireless pressure and strain sensing using finger movement. The control experiment was performed without the H2O2 and visible irradiation.

(a) Confocal image of the DSCD hydrogel after implanting NCD and HFD hepatocytes. (b) EIS resistance, (c) Wireless resistance, (d) LCR pressure response of the DSCD hydrogel after NCD and HFD primary cell treatment. The expression level of ROS-regulated genes in (e) NCD or (f) HFD hepatocytes implanted in PVA-HPC or DSCD hydrogel without (W/O) and with visible light treatment (W/Vis). The expression level of lipogenesis-related genes in (g) NCD or (h) HFD hepatocytes implanted in PVA-HPC or DSCD hydrogel without (W/O) and with visible light treatment (W/ Vis). (n = 3, ns is not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001)

(a) The schematic diagram represents the implantation of the hydrogel in iWAT. After 4 weeks, the implant was eliminated, and wireless pressure sensing was evaluated. (b) EIS resistance, (c) wireless resistance, (d) LCR pressure response of the implanted hydrogel. (e) Histological images of H&E stained iWAT (upper) and liver (lower) sections. Scale bar = 100 µm for liver, Scale bar = 200 µm for adipose tissue. (f) Oil red O staining of frozen liver sections. Scale bar = 100 µm. (g) Representation of the ORO positive area. W/O and W/ represent without and with visible light treatment, respectively. (n = 3, *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001)

head.ce:title: Stimuli-responsive pressure–strain sensor-based conductive hydrogel for alleviated non-alcoholic fatty liver disease by scavenging reactive oxygen species in adipose tissue

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A visible light- and reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive pressure/strain sensor based on carbon dot (CD)-loaded conductive hydrogel was developed for detecting high-fat diet (HFD) and preventing the risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. The designed nanoparticle consisted of a diselenide polymer dot (dsPD) loaded with a visible light-responsive CD to form dsPD@CD (DSCD). The influence of visible light irradiation and ROS on DSCD facilitated the electron transport, enhancing the conductivity of DSCD-embedded hydrogel (DSCD hydrogel) from 1.3 to 35.9 mS/m. Alternatively, the tensile modulus of the DSCD hydrogel enhanced to 223 % after light-induced ROS treatment, which simultaneously impacted the capacitive response (120 %). The hydrogel implantation into inguinal white adipose tissue of HFD mice showed 82 % higher conductivity and 83 % enhanced pressure sensing response to HFD-generated high ROS levels compared with the normal diet-fed mice. Additionally, the ROS scavenging activity of DSCD hydrogel was confirmed by the downregulation of ROS-responsive genes, such as Sod2, Nrf2, and catalase (Cat) in murine primary hepatocytes isolated from fatty liver-induced mice. In addition, in vivo animal studies also confirmed the suppression of hepatic lipogenesis, as shown by decreased Pparγ and Fasn expression and hypertrophy of adipocytes in HFD mice. The distinguishable real-time wireless resistance response observed with pressure sensing indicates the potential application of the device for monitoring the risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.

A visible-light-induced ROS-responsive carbon dot-loaded conductive hydrogel was developed for the detection of HFD-induced alterations in ROS levels by evaluating the conductivity and electrochemical responses with applied pressure/strain. The implanted hydrogel facilitates the recovery of the inflated adipocytes induced by NAFLD, which reduces fat accumulation in the liver, preventing the risk of NAFLD. Real-time detection based on the resistance response during local compression of the hydrogel is possibly performed utilizing a wireless sensing device, demonstrating the ease of NAFLD monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-alcoholic fatty liver is mostly caused by obesity and significant alterations in adipose tissue. Obesity caused by malnutrition causes adipose tissue hypertrophy or hyperplasia, which leads to lipotoxicity by altering the circulating free fatty acids (FFA), a critical event in the etiology of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) [1–3]. Increased FFAs from adipose tissue circulate and are transported to the liver, where they are esterified to triglycerides by de novo lipogenesis or catalyzed by fatty acid oxidation inside the hepatocytes [4,5]. Moreover, excessive FFA accumulation in white adipose tissue increases reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and can play a central role in the development and progression of NAFLD through the reprogramming of hepatic lipid metabolism and modulation of inflammation [6,7]. Considering the complexity of the liver environment and the variety of etiologies, there is an urgent need to develop anti-inflammatory and ROS-induced fat reduction-based detection and treatment methods for NAFLD [8–10]. A plausible method for decreasing ROS-induced fat accumulation would involve sensing ROS by administrating ROS scavengers [11,12]. In this scenario, the stimuli-responsive hydrogel sensor has garnered significant interest owing to its unique characteristics of sensing diverse biochemical signals, which can facilitate environment-responsive treatment, modulate cell behavior, and aid in disease diagnosis [13–16]. In time, the hydrogel can be used locally to sustainably deliver ROS scavengers to target sites in a controlled therapeutic manner. Alternatively, because of the resemblance of mechanical properties between hydrogels and human skin, hydrogels are widely used as wearable pressure-sensing devices for health monitoring systems [17]. Zhang et al. fabricated a ROS-responsive hydrogel based on pullulan for detection of ROS through optical evaluation, and Aran et al. prepared an electrode based on a hydrogel polymer that contains thiocarbamate that was cleaved after contact with ROS, changing the impedance in the process [18,19]. The use of hydrogel in detecting ROS has recently become prevalent. However, because of the minimum stimuli-responsive behavior of the hydrogel one detection technique that uses the correlation between mechanical deformation and electrical signal, especially pressure of strain sensing, remains unexplored. Therefore, developing a conductive hydrogel-based pressure–strain sensor with a distinct sensing response to intracellular ROS concentration in high-fat adipocytes will be beneficial for detecting and monitoring NAFLD. Furthermore, the ROS scavenging activity will be an added advantage for treating NAFLD.

To confer hydrogels with advantageous mechanical properties, conductivity, sensitivity, and stability for a wide application range and stimuli-responsive behavior, the inclusion of carbon and polymer dots has demonstrated potent effects, leading to desired advantages attributed to the presence of graphitic sp2 domain, tunable fluorescence, and abandoned functional groups [20]. For instance, Park et al. recently studied glutathione (GSH)-sensitive conductive polymer dots that can distinguish that can distinguish normal and cancer cells from one another by emitting a unique electrical signal in reaction to a strain or pressure applied on the hydrogel [14]. Hence, it is expected that the design of a ROS-sensitive/ROS-scavenging CD for pressure-strain sensing hydrogel matrices will be beneficial in detecting abnormal ROS concentrations in high-fat adipocytes that elicit a distinct electrochemical response for the detection and treatment of NAFLD.

In this study, we developed a visible light-induced ROS-responsive CD (DSCD)-loaded conductive hydrogel (DSCD hydrogel) for the simple detection of normal and high-fat diet (HFD)-induced alterations in ROS levels by evaluating the conductivity and electrochemical responses with applied pressure/strain. The diverse features of the DSCD hydrogel are attributed to changes in on/off fluorescence behavior with controlled resistance of the DSCD in an environment with high ROS levels. In addition, the DSCD hydrogels revealed a therapeutic function that enabled the recovery of the inflated adipocytes induced by NAFLD to regulate lipogenesis. This, in turn, reduced the fat accumulation in the liver. Furthermore, the real-time resistance response of pressure sensing was recorded utilizing a wireless sensing device, demonstrating the ease of NAFLD monitoring.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (f-Si), titanium dioxide (TiO2), selenium, hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC), hydroxypolyvinylpyrrolidone, (PVP, Mw∼55,000), 2-Chloro-3′,4′-dihydroxyacetophenone (CCDP), (3-chloropropyl) triethoxysilane, diethyl ether, and ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. The visible light-induced hydrophobic–hydrophilic tunable TiO2-immobilized fluoro CD, f-cPDA/TiO2 (CD) was synthesized according to the protocol demonstrated in our previous report with minor modifications (detailed experimentation can be found in supporting information) [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DSCD-loaded PVA-HPC hydrogels (DSCD hydrogel)
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 25 mg of DSCD was dispersed in a mixture of double distilled water (DDW, 4 mL) and ethanol (1 mL). Then, 50 mg of HPC was added to the DSCD solution, followed by stirring at 70 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 0.5 g of PVA was added with an increase in the temperature to 90 °C, followed by stirring for another 3 h. The solution was stirred after eliminating the heat source until all bubbles disappeared. The solution was then cast into a petri dish mold, followed by three freeze-thaw cycles −50 °C for 18 h and 25 °C for 6 h). All preparation methods were conducted by preventing irradiation by the light source (dark).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring pressure and strain response using a wireless sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Wireless equipment was prepared to determine the pressure and strain response using a customized electronic circuit connected to an Arduino Uno microcontroller and AppGosu Bluetooth module. The copper wire electrodes from the hydrogel terminal were connected to the circuit. The data were acquired as a real-time graph (as resistance value) with a smartphone via Bluetooth connection. For in vitro treated hydrogel, the DSCD hydrogel was measured after 24 h treatment with normal chow diet (NCD) and HFD hepatocytes. A control experiment was performed without light irradiation (dark).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo electrochemical and pressure sensing performance of DSCD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: All animal research was conducted with the permission of the Wonkwang University Animal Care and Use Committee and in accordance with the institutional guidelines (#WKU21-07). Mice were housed under controlled environmental conditions (22–23 °C temperature; 12 h light/dark cycle). In addition, 8-week-old C57BL/6 male mice were either fed a NCD and a HFD (60 kcal% fat; D12492, Research Diet) for 8 weeks to induce obesity. The mice were divided into six groups (n = 3): HFD PVA-HPC hydrogel i) with and ii) without visible light treatment, HFD DSCD hydrogel iii) with and iv) without visible light treatment, and NCD DSCD hydrogel v) with and vi) without visible light treatment. The implanted hydrogel samples were sliced into sections with dimensions of 0.4 cm (L) × 0.4 cm (W) × 0.2 cm (H). For visible light treatment, the samples were exposed to visible light for 9 h before implantation. All mice were anesthetized, and hydrogels were implanted in the inguinal adipose tissues (iWAT) of the mice. After 4 weeks of diet challenge, all mice fed an HFD and NCD were euthanized, and the implanted hydrogel samples were extracted. The implanted hydrogels were gently washed with water to eliminate any adhered tissue. The samples were then connected with a copper wire to measure resistance using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at a frequency range of 10−2–105 Hz, 10 mV open-circuit voltage, and a wireless sensing system. The corresponding pressure sensing performance was evaluated in the same hydrogel sample by connecting it to the LCR meter (at 1 kHz frequency and 1 V direct current) and wireless sensing system by gently pressing the sample with the tip of an index finger.

The mean value and standard deviation were calculated for the data representation and error bars of each measurement. Statistical significance was indicated as ns when not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 and performed using t-tests: paired two samples for means with the following inputs: tail(s) = two; α error probability = 0.05. All values in the data were obtained from three measurements (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of visible light- and ROS-responsive DSCD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: A visible light and ROS tunable conductivity of a hydrogel with a controllable fluorescence on/off property of CD was developed for wireless pressure/strain sensing to monitor the HFD-generated abnormal metabolic pathways in adipose tissue. The sensing mechanism was based on CD release from diselenide-crosslinked-dsPD in response to high ROS concentrations typically observed in NAFLD. Simultaneously, the ROS scavenging activity of the fabricated DSCD hydrogel delivered the therapeutic function, facilitating the revival of the inflation of adipocytes induced by NAFLD. As illustrated in Scheme S1, the visible light- and ROS-responsive DSCD was obtained by loading CD nanoparticles with diselenide-crosslinked-dsPD via hydrophobic interactions and π–π stacking [22]. The TiO2 was integrated into the CD to improve the absorption range to visible light by decreasing the band gap of the system [23]. Therefore, in the presence of visible light, the photocatalytic effect promoted by TiO2 reduced the particle size of the CD and induced hydrophobic to hydrophilic transformation, which eventually adjusted the properties of the DSCD-prepared system. An excessive amount of ROS is produced in adipocytes upon consumption of HFD [24] To mimic the ROS microenvironment, 0.1 mM H2O2 was utilized to test the hypothesis [25]. In the presence of 0.1 mM H2O2, cleavage of a diselenide bond occurs in dsPD, triggering the release of CDs. When the diselenide bond is broken by H2O2, the hydrophobicity of dsPD is further reduced, thereby facilitating the release of CD nanoparticles. Furthermore, the release of CDs with the cleavage of dsPD enhanced the conductivity and pressure/strain sensing response of DSCD hydrogel under high ROS conditions compared with that under normal conditions, which can be readily checked via a smartphone using a wireless sensor device. The liberated particles create a strong interfacial hydrogen bond with the CD-contained polydopamine (PDA) catechol groups, which enhances the mechanical strength and stretchability of the DSCD hydrogel. The designed DSCD hydrogel was utilized to decrease the oxidative stress attributed to the ROS-scavenging ability of DSCD. This triggered the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines in iWAT, which controlled ROS accumulation by regulating lipolysis in adipocytes to reduce free fatty acid production in the liver. Thus, increased fat metabolism reduced the risk of NAFLD (Fig. 1 a).

The characteristics of the particles were observed by recording the dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectra under different conditions (Fig. 1b). The loaded nanoparticles of DSCD exhibited a size of 532 nm. However, in the presence of visible light, the size of the CD particles was reduced to 436 nm; this was attributed to the photocatalytic effect [26]. After 9 h of treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2, smaller-sized particles (75 and 338 nm) were observed due to the disruption of diselenide bonds in dsPD and the release of the CDs. However, 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 produced the smallest-sized particles measuring 72 and 325 nm, and was attributed to the added photocatalytic effect (Scheme S1). Figs. 1c and S1 depict the fluorescence emission of DSCD nanoparticles detected using photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The hydrophobic interaction between CD and dsPD and π–π stacking suppressed the DSCD fluorescence, resulting in the fluorescence “off” state with induced aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [27]. The emission peak observed at 505 nm was detected at an excitation wavelength of 465 nm in all the cases after individual treatment of DSCD with visible light, H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. The fluorescence was switched to the “on” state because of the breaking of the diselenide bonds and the release of CDs. The enhancement of the intensity of PL observed after the treatment process indicated enhanced electron transport within the particles after visible light-triggered ROS treatment. The cleavage of the diselenide bond in response to 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 was confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 1d, S2). In Fig. S2b, the peaks at 57.8 eV corresponding to diselenide (SeSe) were observed with the DSCD before treatment. However, after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, the peak corresponding to SeSe was not present, and a new peak of SeO was observed at 59.5 eV (Fig. 1d).

The concentration of H2O2 on the DSCD hydrogel was optimized by evaluating the EIS resistance with varying concentrations of H2O2, ranging from 0 to 1 mM, and a treatment duration of 9 h (Fig. S3a). Based on EIS results, it is evident that the resistance values changed rapidly with the increase of H2O2 concentration, but after that, they did not show any significant reduction between concentrations of 0.1 and 1 mM. A similar observation was also experienced with the confocal images of the DSCD hydrogel-coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate, where the fluorescence intensity drastically enhanced from 5 µM to 0.1 mM H2O2 concentration but did not show any significant improvement between the concentrations of 0.1 mM and 1 mM (Fig. S3b). These observations can be attributed to the enhanced disassembly of DSCD particles with increased H2O2 concentration. After reaching an H2O2 concentration of 0.1 mM with 9 h of treatment, it was assumed that most DSCD particles had disintegrated and reached the threshold point. As a result, no significant decrease in resistance or increase in fluorescence was observed after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2. The DSCD hydrogel-coated PET substrate did not reveal any fluorescence (“off” state) when examined using a confocal microscope. However, upon individual treatment with visible light, H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, a gradual increase in the fluorescence (“on” state) was observed, which further supports the ACQ phenomenon (Fig. S4). To support the argument about the disintegration of the diselenide bond and the release of the CD, the release profile of the loaded nanoparticles was measured (Fig. S5). In the presence of visible light and H2O2, the CD was measured at a higher release amount of 24.37 % after 1 week compared with those without treatment, suggesting that H2O2 has a positive effect on the hydrogel.

Further confirmation of the changes in DSCD morphology was obtained using transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 1e and f). The particle sizes decreased drastically from 468 to 258 nm after 9 h of simultaneous visible light and H2O2 treatment, which was compatible with the DLS result. Additionally, the selected area electron diffraction pattern showed that 100 and 002 planes of the DSCD correspond to the sp2 carbon-rich graphitic nature of the DSCDs. The more fringe patterns observed after simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 were attributed to the redistribution of particles, suggesting a better distribution of sp2 carbon units [28]. The X-ray diffraction study revealed that the diffraction pattern predominated in the TiO2 particle structure due to higher crystallinity. The broad peak centered at 18.2° was related to the 002 plane of the sp2 domain of CDs (Fig. S6) [29]. The more intense 002 plane could be attributed to increased crystallinity after simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 [30]. The distribution of the particles within the hydrogel matrix was observed using atomic force microscopy (AFM), which showed that the deeper roughness of the DSCD hydrogel was almost similar to that of the nanoparticle size observed before 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 (Fig. 1g, S7a). A trend of gradual reduction was observed after 9 h of individual treatment with visible light and H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 from the depth profile (Fig. 1h, S7b). The contact angle changed from 72.6° to 62.3° after visible light treatment attributed to visible light-induced photocatalytic activity (Figure S8). However, after 9 h of H2O2 treatment and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, the contact angle reached the values of 37.8° and 28.3°, respectively. This is due to the release of CDs and SeO bonds generated after the cleavage of dsPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology, optical, electrical, and mechanical properties of DSCD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: EIS was performed to verify the visible light-assisted ROS responsiveness of DSCD hydrogel (Figs. 2 a, S9). The resistance value was reduced upon 9 h of treatment with visible light, H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, resulting in the increase of conductivity values from 1.3 mS/m (control) to 5.8, 11.6, and 35.9 mS/m, respectively. These results can be attributed to the simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, which leads to a uniform distribution of the conducting phase upon the release of CDs and cleavage of dsPD, thereby generating smaller-sized hydrophilic particles. This facilitates the electron transfer process mediated by charge carriers with increased conductivity generated in the DSCD hydrogel [31]. The source meter resistance of the DSCD hydrogel-coated Si-wafer demonstrated a similar reduction, demonstrating a more continuous conducting phase compared with aggregated particles, indicating that the release of CDs had an effect on the conductivity (Fig. S10). Furthermore, wireless detection of the resistance confirmed the applicability of our designed method for real-time operation (Fig. S11). Moreover, the DSCD hydrogel also demonstrated altered swelling behavior depending on the treatment condition (Fig. S12a). Initially, the DSCD hydrogel showed a weak swelling behavior (98 %) due to a hydrophobic association of the nanoparticles. The swelling was improved (174 %) after exposure to visible light due to the photocatalytic hydrophilic transformation. Furthermore, with 9 h of treatment with H2O2, the released CDs and generation of a SeO bond increased the hydrophilicity, which further increased the swelling to 322 %. Finally, with 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, the combined effect showed the highest swelling of 378 %. The water content of the DSCD hydrogel during swelling and storage at room temperature was measured (Fig. S12b). The hydrogel water content varied according to the stimuli wherein the water content decreased faster for DSCD hydrogel treated with visible irradiation and H2O2 treatment compared with other treatments.

Fig. 2b presents stress–strain curves of the DSCD hydrogel depending on treatment conditions. The Young's modulus of DSCD hydrogel increased from 68 to 238 kPa, demonstrating an approximately 3.5-fold increase after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 (Fig. S13). The visual stretchability was extended up to 333 % of its original length without cracking after 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, corresponding to the enhancement of the molecular network inside the hydrogel (Fig. 2c). Moreover, Fig. 2d shows the dependency of the viscoelastic moduli (G′ and G″) on the applied shear strain (γ) at a given frequency of 1 Hz. The release of CD particles from DSCD corresponded to higher moduli, demonstrated a significant improvement in the linear viscoelastic region from 36 % to 68 %, and increased yield strain. The released PDA particles formed a strong interfacial hydrogen bond caused by catechol groups, enhancing the DSCD hydrogel's mechanical strength and elasticity. Furthermore, we assessed the self-recovery of DSCD hydrogels by alternate-step strain measurement performed by continuously varying the strain between 0.1 % and 30 % at the same frequency (1 Hz). The G′ of DSCD hydrogel without treatment could not be recovered after strain application, whereas, after 9 h of treatment with visible light and large strain, the storage modulus (G') was remarkably reduced, with a value lower than the loss modulus (G″) (Fig. 2e). Notwithstanding, the G′ was almost restored to its strength value immediately after the applied strain was changed to 0.1 %. After 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, the G′ and G″ remained unchanged in the strain range of 0.1–30 %. This finding demonstrates that the DSCD hydrogel can recover mechanical strength due to reversible hydrogen bond formation after the release of CDs from dsPD.

Additionally, the porous structures of the freeze-dried DSCD hydrogel were observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope. Compared with the original DSCD hydrogel (Fig. S14a), the microstructure of DSCD hydrogel after 9 h of visible light treatment (Fig. S14b) and 9 h of simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 (Fig. S14c) showed higher density and smaller pores. The adjustment in pore size by the ROS was measured using CO2 adsorption isotherm (Fig. S15). The surface area of DSCD hydrogel was decreased from 43.36 m2/g to 43.18, 33.42, and 20.30 m2/g after visible irradiation, H2O2 treatment, and both treatments, respectively (Table S2). The shift in the pore was affected by the cleavage of SeSe that simultaneously discharged the loaded CD, resulting in the formation of uniformly dispersed particles for hydrogen bonding association, further improving the structural integrity of the cross-linked network. A macroscopic self-healing tensile recovery test was performed to evaluate the self-healing capability of the DSCD hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 2f, the DSCD hydrogels were cut sliced into rectangular shapes, and after 6 h of H2O2 treatment or simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, they self-healed when the two sections of the hydrogel came into contact and were left undisturbed at room temperature. After healing, no apparent physical damage was observed at the junction. The self-healed DSCD hydrogel could withstand a strain up to ∼585 % with 195 kPa Young's modulus and showed 82 % recovery of its mechanical strength after self-healing (Fig. 2g). The adhesive strength of the DSCD hydrogel was assessed, showing an attachment force of 0.42, 0.56, and 0.65 N for control, visible irradiated, and simultaneous H2O2 and visible light treatment (Fig. S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical resistance changes in response to mechanical deformation (tensile and compression)
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the generation of hydrogen bonds after simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, differential scanning calorimetric (DSC) analysis was performed. The DSC findings of the gels revealed two usual physical transitions: glass transition temperature (T g) and melting temperature (T m) (Fig. S17). The T g of the DSCD hydrogel shifted from 108 to 113 °C. Simultaneously, the T m was also enhanced from 221 to 229 °C because of the increased strength and hydrogen bonds after the disruption of DSCD by simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of the DSCD hydrogel (Fig. S18) revealed a change in the OH stretching vibration peak from 3416 to 3447 cm–1, which demonstrates the effect of the hydrogen bond promoted by CDs released from dsPD after cleavage upon treatment of DSCD nanoparticles with H2O2 [32]. Due to its ultra-stretchability, recoverability, remarkable sensitivity, and quick reaction capacity, the DSCD hydrogel has much potential for wearable sensing devices (Fig. S19a). Fig. 3 a plots the DSCD hydrogel strain sensor's relative electrical capacitance change ratio (ΔC/C0) against the applied strain [33]. The strain sensing performance associated with DSCD hydrogels upon simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2 showed higher sensitivity. The distance between the conductive particles expanded as the DSCD hydrogel sensors were stretched, resulting in decreased conductive routes and increased capacitance [34]. A strain sensitivity gauge factor of 0.52 was obtained for the DSCD hydrogel and reached the values of 1.15 and 1.82 after treatment with H2O2 and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, respectively [35]. Figs. 3b and S19b show the graphs of capacitance change for the conductive DSCD hydrogel under various tensile stresses. The capacitance of the hydrogel increased progressively with stretching as the strain was increased, and the capacitance was almost restored to the starting value once the strain was relaxed. These results demonstrated the sensitivity of the DSCD hydrogel to high strain [36]. The results were also supported by resistance measurement, showing an increase in the resistance change with the increase of strain, which is associated with increased electron transfer distance after being stretched (Figure S19c).

We assembled a sensor by sealing a piece of hydrogel within the very high bond tape to perform the bending motions (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, when the finger was maintained at the same angle, the ΔC/C0 remained constant. Finally, when the finger was straightened, the capacitance value was restored to its normal value. Additionally, when the index finger was bent from a straightened condition, the relative capacitance change response signals rose synchronously, and ΔC/C0 displayed good repeatability throughout the cyclic process of bending and straightening, showing exceptional reversibility and rapid reaction (Fig. 3d) [37]. Similarly, reproducible responses of the hydrogel were observed when pressure was applied by the finger (Fig. 3e). The pressure sensing response was recorded by applying pressures ranging from 0.1–40 kPa (Fig. S20a), and showed a similar trend with the finger-pressing response of the DSCD hydrogel. When the pressure increased, the sensor was deformed with the applied pressure, and its capacitance change pinpointed an enhanced response depending on the treatment condition. Additionally, the hydrogel displayed reversibility during force implementation cyclically and repeatedly (Fig. S20b).

Moreover, the sensor durability was further examined by measuring the relative capacitance change observed after 3000 finger bending cycles (Figs. 3f, S20c, and S20d). The sensor showed 72 % retention of the response without treatment, while the retention ratio increased to 78 %, 83 %, and 85 % after individual treatment with visible light, H2O2, and simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, respectively. The capacitance amplitude of the pressure sensing process for treatment conditions was enhanced by 102 %, 115 %, and 120 % for the respective treatment conditions. The output signal quality achieved via wireless and traditional wired transmissions were compared (Figs. S20e and S20f). Because of the wireless analysis measurement data associated with the change in the resistivity of the hydrogel, the electrical resistance of the DSCD hydrogel was measured, showing an increase in resistance with the increase in bending angle and pressure. Fig. 3g depicts the hydrogel sensor's real-time sensing performance with finger bending and pressure performance, which also showed a similar response depending on the pressure and the strain. The previous experiments provided sufficient evidence of ROS sensitivity, demonstrated by altering the physical, mechanical, and electrochemical performance with H2O2 treatment. Hence, we used our DSCD hydrogel for further investigation in an in vivo and in vitro model to study the effect of ROS levels on the DSCD hydrogel to reduce the risk of NAFLD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro study of DSCD hydrogel using NCD and HFD hepatocytes
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of HFD-induced ROS generation and their consequences in vitro, murine primary hepatocytes isolated from the livers of NCD and HFD mice were embedded in the hydrogel, and the relative change in the behavior of the hydrogels was studied. NCD hepatocytes did not show any obvious change in the fluorescence behavior of the DSCD particles using a confocal microscope for the DSCD hydrogel, whereas the HFD hepatocytes showed a considerable change in fluorescence due to the presence of high amounts of ROS (Fig. 4 a). The EIS (Fig. 4b) and source meter resistance (Fig. S21a) of the DSCD hydrogel support that HFD hepatocytes showed a significant change in resistance value compared with the NCD hepatocytes. The HFD hepatocytes released a high amount of ROS compared with NCD hepatocytes, which catalyze the breaking of diselenide bonds more effectively after visible light treatment, and, as a result, the decrease in the resistance value is more prominent for HFD hepatocytes treated with DSCD hydrogel. The conductivity value increased from 1.7 to 2.6 mS/m and 4.2 to 8.6 mS/m for NCD and HFD hepatocytes treated with DSCD hydrogel, respectively, as calculated from the EIS spectra. The trend in the resistance value was also verified using a wireless detection method (Fig. 4c). Because of the alteration of the ROS-responsive conductivity and mechanical property, pressure and strain sensing response using LCR (Fig. 4d) and wireless sensing method (Fig. S21b) showed an incremental response for HFD hepatocytes treated with DSCD hydrogel compared with NCD after visible light treatment.

To prove that the effect of ROS is solely regulated by the DSCD, a controlled experiment was conducted using a pristine PVA-HPC hydrogel (without the incorporation of DSCD). The PVA-HPC hydrogel did not show any response to change in resistance (Fig. S22a–c) as well as pressure and strain sensing (Fig. S22d and e) depending on the different ROS microenvironment with visible light treatment. Additionally, the DCFH-DA was performed to investigate the ROS scavenging performance of DSCD hydrogel using Hep2 cells induced with 1× ROS inducer. Endogenous ROS levels of cells cultured with a ROS inducer increased, and introducing the DSCD hydrogel could alleviate intercellular ROS accumulation (Fig. S23). To analyze the effect of DSCD on the ROS and hepatic lipid accumulation, the related gene expression was analyzed. One of the primary cellular antioxidant pathways that counter ROS is mediated by nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) [38]. The translocation of activated Nrf2 to the nucleus upregulates the expression of phase 2 enzyme genes, including Cat and superoxide dismutase 2 (Sod2) [39]. The results show that DSCD hydrogel after visible light treatment significantly downregulated Sod2, Nrf2, and Cat after 24 h incubation in NCD and HFD primary hepatocytes, indicating DSCD could directly scavenge intracellular ROS and resist oxidative damage by down expression of antioxidant genes (Fig. 4e and f) [40]. The regression of ROS gene expression is more prominent for NCD primary cell treated DSCD hydrogel. This is because the DSCD particles present within the hydrogel are sufficient to catalyze all the diselenide bonds present in lower ROS levels. In contrast, for HFD, the DSCD particles present within the hydrogel is not sufficient enough to catalyze all the diselenide bonds, particularly under high ROS levels. Moreover, the downregulated expression of antioxidant genes proves the intercellular ROS scavenging activity of the DSCD hydrogel. Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-gamma (PPARγ), the master regulators of de novo lipogenesis, and fatty acid synthase (FASN), a critical enzyme in the biosynthesis of fatty acids are emerged as key regulators in hepatic lipid metabolism. As illustrated in Fig. 4g and h, the expression level of Pparγ and Fasn was significantly reduced in HFD hepatocytes implanted in DSCD hydrogel after the visible light exposure compared with NCD and PVA-HPC hydrogel. These observations suggest that DSCD hydrogel with visible light treatment alleviates NAFLD by regulating lipogenesis and fatty acid oxidation in HFD primary hepatocytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study of ROS scavenging behavior and free fatty acid reduction performance of DSCD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether the ROS scavenging behavior of DSCD hydrogel could be employed as an effective therapeutic strategy for detecting and preventing NAFLD, the hydrogels were implanted into the iWAT of HFD-fed mice. HFD-induced fat accumulation led to ROS generation and increased adipokine and tumor necrosis factor-alpha release, encouraging chronic inflammation [4]. Fig. 5 a illustrates the implantation of DSCD hydrogel into iWAT of mice fed an HFD. The resistance of HFD mice declined in comparison to the resistance of NCD mice, which can be attributed to the disruption of the SeSe bond of dsPD with the increase in ROS concentration in adipocytes that increased the mobility, as well as electron transport of the CD to intensify the conductivity of the hydrogel (Fig. 5b). Moreover, it is noteworthy that after irradiation with visible light, the enhancement of the conductivity of the DSCD hydrogel implanted in HFD mice was much higher (4.5–8.2 mS/m). This enhanced conductivity was evidenced by simultaneous treatment with visible light and H2O2, which combined the photocatalytic effect of CDs released with cleavage of dsPD at a high concentration of ROS. In the case of NCD mice, the ROS concentration was comparatively low, demonstrating a comparatively lower increment in conductivity (1.6–2.8 mS/m); rather, it showed the normal trend observed after treatment with visible irradiation only.

Furthermore, the resistance value was measured from the source meter, which validated that the DC resistance also followed the conductivity trend of EIS (Fig. S24a). The changes in the resistance of implanted hydrogels were then characterized and monitored using a wireless system, which also attributed to the low resistance signals of the visible light-treated HFD mice implanted DSCD hydrogel compared with the other hydrogels; the results were consistent with the EIS data (Fig. 5c). Pressure sensing was performed using the wireless system to monitor the electrochemical response of the hydrogel in response to ROS scavenging behavior in mice. The results showed a minimal change in the sensing behavior of hydrogels implanted in NCD mice (Fig. S24b). However, the DSCD hydrogels implanted in HFD mice exhibited a more intense response after irradiation with visible light, which could be explained by enhanced conductivity and mechanical properties due to the release of CDs after the cleavage of dsPD. The LCR pressure response also demonstrated the highest response amplitude for the DSCD hydrogel treated with visible light implanted in the HFD mice (Fig. 5d). As a control experiment, to examine the effect of the DSCD hydrogel on ROS scavenging behavior, we implanted a non-conducting PVA-HPC hydrogel without DSCD, performed conductivity measurements (Fig. S25a and b), and analyzed the pressure sensing response (Fig. S25c and d). This showed no changes in the resistance value or the pressure-sensing response under the same treatment condition. These results demonstrate that the DSCD was responsible for the ROS scavenging behavior and was associated with the electrochemical response. The NCD mice with normal ROS levels in iWAT showed no difference when implanted with DSCD hydrogels with and without visible irradiation (Fig. S26). In contrast, as shown in Fig. 5e, hypertrophy of adipocyte in iWAT and accumulation of hepatic lipids decreased in HFD mice implanted with DSCD hydrogel compared with that in non-treated mice (PVA-HPC hydrogel), suggesting that the ROS scavenging ability of DSCD hydrogel effectively reduced HFD-induced ROS accumulation and adipogenesis in iWAT and decreased the accumulation of hepatic lipids. Upon irradiation with visible light, the DSCD hydrogel showed higher therapeutic ability to reduce the hypertrophy of adipocytes in iWAT. This reduction was possibly induced by abnormal ROS concentration, implying that the hydrophilic transition of the release of CD reduced the sizes of the particles and increased the distribution of the nanoparticles in the hydrogel to enhance the ROS scavenging ability. The hypertrophy of adipocytes and hepatic lipid droplets was significantly increased with implantation of the PVA-HPC hydrogel into iWAT of HFD mice, whereas a significant decrease in the accumulation of lipid droplets was observed with the implantation of the DSCD hydrogel into iWAT of HFD mice. This suggests the potential therapeutic ability of our DSCD hydrogel to regulate hepatic lipid metabolism.

Furthermore, the distribution of fat demonstrated by the Oil Red O stained droplets (Fig. 5f) also confirmed the reduction in the fat accumulation with the implantation of DSCD hydrogel into iWAT of HFD mice upon irradiation with visible light. Fig. 5g demonstrates the quantification of the Oil Red O positive area, plotted based on the implantation of DSCD hydrogel with and without visible irradiation. The results also supported the possible therapeutic ability of the DSCD hydrogel in the development and progression of NAFLD in the presence of visible light. Furthermore, the degradability and shelf-life of the DSCD hydrogel were studied using two methods: in vitro immersion in various types of serum and an in vivo test on NCD and HFD mice at a specific period. Fig. S27 shows that after 4 weeks of immersion in FBS, BSA, horse serum, and human serum, the mechanical performance and electrical signal of DSCD hydrogel did not significantly change. In vivo analysis of the hydrogel showed a reduction in the mass ratio of the hydrogel following implantation in HFD mice, which was influenced by CD release (Fig. S28). Additionally, the effect of ROS and fatty acid levels on the DSCD hydrogel can be monitored for HFD-treated mice (Fig. S30), showing a decrease in resistance and an enhanced compression modulus. In contrast, NCD-treated mice exhibited little to no shift following implantation for 20 days (Fig. S29). The change in the electrical and stress-strain properties in HFD-treated mice was influenced by the high ROS and fatty acid levels, which facilitated the release of the CD and boosted the mechanical properties.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of DSCD hydrogel, the hydrogels were subcutaneously implanted in mice for up to 20 days to investigate the in vivo inflammation responses and the appearance of the skin in the implantation site. No skin reactions, including swelling, blisters, and ulcers, were observed in the incision side after treatment with DSCD hydrogel. H&E and F4/80 staining showed that the presence of DSCD hydrogel did not induce a severe inflammation response in the skin of the implantation site (Fig. S31). Moreover, to simulate the NAFLD development, the DSCD hydrogels were implanted into the iWAT of NCD mice and changed to HFD for 3 weeks. According to Fig. S32, the hydrogel demonstrated a decrease in resistance and an increase in compression modulus each week owing to the high ROS and fatty acid levels in mice. Furthermore, H&E staining showed that the presence of DSCD hydrogel did not result in a significant increase in adipocyte hypertrophy and hepatic lipid accumulation compared with the control. The influence of ROS on DSCD hydrogel during implantation was investigated using an in vivo imaging system (IVIS). Implantation of hydrogel into iWAT of HFD mice was successfully monitored using IVIS due to the change from fluorescent “OFF” to “ON” involving the release of the CD, as depicted in the time-dependent image in Fig. S33. Various systems that could detect ROS and fatty acids based on the stimulation response have already been reported; however, an integrated system that can give many signal types remains to be explored. From Table S3 in supporting information, most of the systems, whether hydrogel or scaffold, are focused on one type of detection: colorimetric, fluorometric, electrochemistry, ratiometry, or imaging. Herein, we introduced a system that can detect ROS and fatty acids based on the transition in the fluorescent, electrical, and mechanical properties integrated with a system that can connect to a smartphone (wirelessly through Bluetooth). In the end, this integrated system would become a catalyst for advanced detection in the future.

In summary, a visible light-responsive and ROS-stimulated wireless pressure/strain sensing hydrogel with controllable conductivity was developed to evaluate the possible therapeutic potential of NAFLD by reducing HFD-induced abnormal ROS levels in the adipose tissue. Herein, the ROS scavenging activity of the DSCD hydrogel was confirmed by the downregulation of ROS-responsive genes Sod2, Nrf2, and Cat. In addition, the DSCD hydrogel suppressed the hepatic levels of Pparγ and Fasn, accompanied by a decrease in the hypertrophy of adipocytes, resulting in the reduction of hepatic fat accumulation. In the presence of visible light, the designed DSCD was distributed into small particles, and ROS triggered the release of the CDs through the cleavage of diselenide bonds in dsPD within the DSCD hydrogel, leading to 27-fold enhanced conductivity with tunable fluorescence on/off system. The visible light and ROS-induced reinforcement demonstrated 223 % enhancement of the tensile strength, 120 % capacitance response, and 37 % cyclic stability. The HFD mice implanted hydrogel showed superior conductivity (82 %) and enhanced response (83 %) to pressure compared with NCD mice owing to the high ROS levels generated by HFD. The decrease in oxidative stress attributed to the ROS scavenging ability of DSCD inhibited the expansion of adipose tissue and reduced FFA delivery into hepatocytes. As a result, hepatic lipid accumulation was decreased, suggesting a reduced risk of NAFLD. Wireless real-time monitoring of pressure/strain electronic response provides a new concept for designing a hydrogel-based implantable system for diagnostic and therapeutic applications. In this study, we demonstrate the ROS scavenging effects and efficacy of our system against NAFLD.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. (RS-2023-00207925 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 to S.Y Park and 2021R1I1A3041149 to E-J Jin).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.actbio.2023.09.030.
